title: What Do Musicians Do About GenAI? | by Imogen Heap | Medium
text:
What Do Musicians Do About GenAI?
Imogen Heap
7 min read
·
Dec 5, 2025
--
Listen
Share
Major labels are now secretly announcing their first deals with generative AI companies, as I hinted in
my previous Medium article
. This past November, Universal, Sony, and Warner announced a partnership with the AI startup
Klay
with little to no transparency.
These deals feel reminiscent of those made during the DSP (Digital Service Providers) boom years ago, involving companies like Spotify. Back then, the labels negotiated shares in these companies, and in return, the DSPs got to license some of our works. I say “some” because the majority of the music being streamed on DSPs is owned and distributed by independent labels and artists. These licensing deals primarily protect platforms from costly lawsuits by industry giants rather than artists’ rights, given the small percentage of music content they own and control.
For those who are signed by Universal, Sony or Warner, it is very unclear how they plan to handle crediting and compensating their artists through AI model training and output with Klay. Were any of my friends who are signed to these majors contacted directly or made aware that a deal was coming? Those whom I have asked have all responded, “not yet.”
After a little digging
on LinkedIn
, I noticed that one of the co-founders of Klay is the former President of Global Digital Business & US Sales/Distribution at Sony Music, Thomas Hesse.
A press release
sent out by Universal on Oct 28th called Klay an “ethical AI” company due to their willingness to partner with these major labels before training their models. (
Something Suno and Udio failed to do and are paying for now.
) I then dug around to find Klay missing from Ed Newton-Rex’s list of
Fairly Trained Models
.
Klay has yet to launch, but people can join the waiting list for the ambiguous offerings. It’s unclear what will actually become of the company or whether it will hold value after its launch. Meanwhile, established, fully tech-ready companies like
Jenmusic.ai
, which provide a comprehensive solution for ethical generative AI and collaborate directly with licence holders, are overlooked. Jen is a fantastic solution,
tried and tested by myself
and many others, but is left out of the conversation of how to navigate the future of the music industry and GenAI with integrity.
My guess is that it’s because Shara Senderoff,
who I speak very highly of
and am forever impressed by for always pursuing the ethical best when it comes to music and AI, was never the president of Sony Music. According to Shara, “The industry continues to act as if control is the only currency that matters. It isn’t. Vision, ethics, and actual innovation matter more than back-end promises or the illusion of power. Artists deserve a future where technology expands their rights and revenue, not one where they’re leveraged into silence. I’ll continue working with the people who understand that building the next era of music requires contribution, accountability, and imagination.”
If the history of major label DSP deals teaches us anything about the future of GenAI and the music industry, it’s likely that musicians will be treated as unpaid afterthoughts. When Spotify went public,
Taylor Swift spoke up
and asked her label, UMG, to give some of their enormous stock earnings back to their artists, non-recoupable. Universal complied and distributed a portion of their $830 million Spotify shares. When word got out,
Sony decided to follow suit
, giving a portion of the 50% of shares they sold in 2018, amounting to $768 million, back to artists (including myself). Despite the monetary hit the labels faced from being leveraged to do the right thing, they are still attempting to cash in, this time perhaps on the ground floor of what’s next in AI tech, hoping for another financial windfall. Few artists possess the clout and catalogue she had years ago to demand fairness, and only a small portion of musicians benefited from those stock sales.
While it is positive (in theory) to see AI startups asking for permission rather than forgiveness in their model training methods, the Klay deal seems more like an attempt by these majors to own the means of production and save face in the process. It also completely ignores the independent artists, like myself, who have little to no leverage or voice as these new platforms emerge, having been trained off our work. This is proving a little too close for comfort for me personally, not having any direct means of representation, though not for lack of trying. I’m still waiting for Universal to ask my permission for these deals regarding my Frou Frou album, “Details”, or even just to have a nice chat about it.
Musicians have found themselves gnawing on the scraps left for us after the majors took their slices of streaming services for the last 20 years and have watched as they set embarrassingly low rates with no tangible way to fight back. Organisations such as the
FAC (Featured Artist Coalition)
have been instrumental in advocating for fairer royalties and ethical practices with new emerging AI technologies, and few others are fighting for us.
My friend Chris Bell, former head of Worldwide Product & Content Marketing for iTunes for 11 years, reached out asking if I had seen
Tom Breihan’s article on Stereogum
about this deal. “Back in 2003, the idea that the entire corpus of recorded music could be scraped off the internet and used to train generative music creation AI was not even a science fiction concept,” he said. “Nor was it considered when the entire industry upgraded audio quality, fidelity, and the digitisation process. The AI era makes Napster look like a high school student science fair project in terms of potential copyright infringement, damages, and loss of revenue for artists and songwriters. The entire recorded music business hangs in the balance.”
Press enter or click to view image in full size
Auracles.io
These GenAI deals, for me, highlight and exemplify the desperate need for an overhaul in how musicians control their digital IP no matter if they are signed or independent. A problem that I and my team have been working on for over a decade. What started in
a Forbes piece
as a blue-sky imagining of what could happen (i.e., what if we built a community of musicians and empowered them to sell their songs as services with information and permissions all intact?) later became the
Creative Passport
, a verified network of musicians that could form the foundation of a new music industry. Now the project has evolved into
Auracles.io
, an autonomous identity and data hub where artists can claim their Auracle ID and begin to catalogue their works. It is a reimagining of how artists could control and manage their digital identity and do business with third-party vendors rather than losing control of their IP the moment it’s uploaded to a distribution company. In this vision, artists are the experts on their work and all related data. Other entities (such as labels, managers, co-writers, studio musicians, producers, etc.) can also contribute information and build a rich and accurate data set that can feed into all kinds of existing and not yet imagined services.
Do you know how many incomplete data sets of works and failed content ID systems exist that are unhelpful, disorganised, and wasteful? My passion for creating Auracles is based on my desire to fix it and empower the data we as individual artists collect, manage, and organise to be open and accessible (based on our personal permissions) to the companies we want to partner with.
These days, artists largely use a major record deal as a stepping stone to independence. Fortunately, numerous artists, both large and small, have successfully made this transition. However, part of the problem independent artists face is obscurity and currently no way to be found by those fans, much less companies that might want to do business with them if that process were easier and more streamlined. We need a machine-readable permissions standard for our songs to reach these services in a way that is time- and cost-saving. We need to create something so essential and profoundly useful that ignoring independent musicians and strictly dealing with the majors would be ludicrous.
We can organise and standardise a system that accounts for the individual artists rather than the companies they are in and out of contracts with throughout their careers. Third-party companies could then operate their business with the IP itself, and in turn, the artists and all those responsible for each recording at a machine level could dictate how the funds are distributed and watch it work automatically. Connecting models to
Auracles.io
through
MCPs
could revolutionise the system by maintaining a clear and fair line of attribution. How else can we let companies know who we are and what we are not willing to do with our IP, both individually and collectively? We need to make it easy for them to do the right thing.
The current music business models (signed or not) require us all to chase down bad actors after we haven’t been paid and/or credited correctly. Very few of us have the resources and time to do this. Artists want to do deals directly because it makes sense for everyone (including the labels). I hope that thanks to some upcoming integrations with Auracles and the overabundant need for a solution to this issue, more attention can be paid to this potential way forward and that more of my peers will come together to create a solution rather than only pointing out the problem. This goal is not a decade-old blue-sky wish anymore. It’s an action plan within our grasp. I hope you agree; chime in, and in the meantime, get your own
Auracle ID
! You can
see mine here
, for example. We have to speak up, or we will be spoken for and lose decades of revenue and proper attribution.
Regardless of whether you choose to join
Auracles.io
in this vision of the future, I hope you’ll take the advice of musician advocates like
Young Music Boss
, who have recently put out a checklist for musicians to use when getting into any deals that may include AI and how to protect themselves.
In the end, we all just want what’s fair. It’s not too much to ask, but we will have to roll up our sleeves and make something ourselves if we want it done right.
Written in collaboration with Uriel Lane – formerly communications and content strategy lead at Megaphonic Records 2025
